coredata.dc:title: Effects of Ti addition on carbon diffusion in molybdenum
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Inoue, Takeshi
Hiraoka, Yutaka
Nagae, Masahiro
Takada, Jun
coredata.dc:description: From the change in fracture mode as a function of distance from the specimen surface, the carbon diffusion distance can be estimated. In addition the diffusion parameter such as activation energy for carbon diffusion in molybdenum can be derived from Arrhenius plots of the carbon diffusion distance and the diffusion temperature. In this work the fracture surface of Mo–Ti alloys after carburizing was examined using a scanning electron microscope. Then the effects of Ti addition on the carbon diffusion behavior in molybdenum were discussed. It was demonstrated, first, the carbon diffusion distance decreases by adding Ti at a given diffusion temperature. Secondly the activation energy for carbon diffusion in Mo–Ti alloys is smaller than that in pure molybdenum. In addition the critical carbon concentration for the transition of fracture mode from intergranular to transgranular in Mo–Ti alloys is larger than that in pure molybdenum. These results are attributed mainly to the preferential combination between a carbon atom and a titanium atom.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metals
Powder metallurgy
Scanning electron microscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic procedures to determine the change in fracture mode as a function of distance from the specimen surface using a photograph.
Definition of carbon diffusion distance from the change in PIF-value as a function of the distance from the surface.
Change in PIF-value as a function of the distance from the surface for the as-recrystallized specimens.
Change in PIF-value as a function of the distance from the surface for the carburized specimens at 1073K.
Change in PIF-value as a function of the distance from the surface for the carburized specimens at 1173K.
Change in PIF-value as a function of the distance from the surface for the carburized specimens at 1273K.
Change in PIF-value as a function of the distance from the surface for the carburized specimens at 1373K.
Change in PIF-value as a function of the distance from the surface for the carburized specimens at 1473K.
Arrhenius plots of the carbon diffusion distance and the diffusion temperature for Mo–Ti alloys as well as Pure Mo.
Lists of diffusion parameters such as the activation energy for carbon diffusion and the constant term log(4β 2 D 0 t) for Mo–Ti alloys and Pure Mo together with the constant β

article.ce:title: Effects of Ti addition on carbon diffusion in molybdenum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Science, Okayama University of Science, 1-1 Ridai-cho, Okayama 700-0005, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Okayama University of Science, 1-1 Ridai-cho, Okayama 700-0005, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Sustainability of Resources, Graduate School of Environmental Science, Okayama University, 3-1-1 Tsushima-naka, Okayama 700-8530, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Chemical and Biological Technology, Graduate School of Natural Science and Technology, Okayama University, 3-1-1 Tsushima-naka, Okayama 700-8530, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: From the change in fracture mode as a function of distance from the specimen surface, the carbon diffusion distance can be estimated. In addition the diffusion parameter such as activation energy for carbon diffusion in molybdenum can be derived from Arrhenius plots of the carbon diffusion distance and the diffusion temperature. In this work the fracture surface of Mo–Ti alloys after carburizing was examined using a scanning electron microscope. Then the effects of Ti addition on the carbon diffusion behavior in molybdenum were discussed. It was demonstrated, first, the carbon diffusion distance decreases by adding Ti at a given diffusion temperature. Secondly the activation energy for carbon diffusion in Mo–Ti alloys is smaller than that in pure molybdenum. In addition the critical carbon concentration for the transition of fracture mode from intergranular to transgranular in Mo–Ti alloys is larger than that in pure molybdenum. These results are attributed mainly to the preferential combination between a carbon atom and a titanium atom.

ce:section.ce:para: Nagae et al. [1–3] examined the hardness change across the specimen thickness in Mo–Ti alloys after heating in NH3 or N2 gaseous atmospheres. They demonstrated that a very hard layer is formed near the specimen surface. This layer has hardness as high as 1600(Hv) or more and thereby is called as “surface hard layer”. They also recognized a relatively hard region just inside the surface hard layer. This region is so-called “internally nitrided region”. A transmission electron microscope (TEM) observation revealed very fine TiN particles dispersed in the matrix. In pure molybdenum subjected to the same heat treatment, however, no internally nitrided region was recognized. These results suggest that a preferred combination between a nitrogen atom and a titanium atom occurred, since the standard free energy of formation of TiN is much more negative than that of Mo2N at the temperature range between 1273 and 1773K. In analog to the internal nitriding phenomena, “internally carburizing” should occur in Mo–Ti alloys which are heated in some carburizing atmospheres, since the standard free energy of formation of TiC is also much more negative than that of Mo2C. From these points of view, Nagae et al. [4] and Inoue et al. [5] examined the hardness change across the specimen thickness in Mo–Ti alloys after carburizing. Nagae et al. heated the specimens in a flowing Ar+CO gaseous atmosphere, whilst Inoue et al. heated the specimens when being buried in graphite powder. In both cases a relatively hard region was recognized just inside the surface hard layer. These results again suggest that a preferred combination between a carbon atom and a titanium atom occurred.

In our previous study [6] we demonstrated that a fractography study using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) is suitable to determine the carbon diffusion distance and consequently to evaluate the activation energy for carbon diffusion in a molybdenum matrix. The activation energy estimated using this method is 182kJ in the temperature range between 1073 and 1173K. This value approximately agrees with previous data [7,8]. In this study we investigated the effects of Ti addition on the carbon diffusion distance in molybdenum and on the diffusion parameters such as activation energy for carbon diffusion. First, the specimens of two dilute Mo–Ti alloys were immersed in a graphite-filled container and heated in vacuum at a temperature between 1073 and 1473K for 1.2ks. The change in fracture mode across the specimen thickness was examined using SEM. Then the carbon diffusion distance was determined at a given diffusion temperature, and the diffusion parameters such as the activation energy for carbon diffusion in molybdenum and the constant term were derived from Arrhenius plots of the carbon diffusion distance and the temperature. The results obtained for Mo–Ti alloys were compared with that for pure molybdenum.

The materials used in this study were sheets of powder metallurgy Mo–Ti alloys. The target Ti contents were 1.0 and 1.5mass%, and designated as “Mo–1.0Ti” and “Mo–1.5Ti”, respectively, in this text. For reference pure molybdenum is designated as “Pure Mo”. The original sheet thickness was about 1mm. From each material three rectangular specimens (3mm wide and 20mm long) were cut out. All the specimen surfaces were polished using emery papers and then subjected to a recrystallization treatment at 1773K for 3.6ks in a vacuum of less than 10−5 Torr. The average grain size of Mo–1.0Ti and Mo–1.5Ti after recrystallization was almost as small as that of Pure Mo (20μm).

Carburization was performed for the recrystallized specimens as follows. The specimens were first buried in graphite powder in a Ta-container. Then the container was heated up to a temperature between 1073 and 1473K with a heating rate of 0.67K/s in vacuum. The container was held for 1.2ks at each temperature and finally cooled down to room temperature.

The carburized specimens as well as the recrystallized ones were fractured in a brittle manner at liquid nitrogen temperature. Two photographs of a magnification of 200 were taken from each fracture surface using SEM. In this study the fracture mode of a material was represented by a ratio of the intergranular fracture area to the total fracture surface (designated as “PIF”-value in this text). For example, the PIF-value for the recrystallized Pure Mo is as high as 90% [6]. This result suggests the grain boundaries are generally very weak. However, these grain boundaries are strengthened by carburizing under an adequate condition and the PIF-value becomes low [6].

The change in fracture mode as a function of distance from the specimen surface is determined as schematically shown in Fig. 1 . The distance from the surface to the center of the specimen is about 500μm and each fractograph is divided into 10 regions as shown in Fig. 1(a). The gray zone denotes transgranular fracture surface(s) (designated as “TG”), whislt the white zone denotes intergranular fracture surface(s) (designated as “IG”). The PIF-value is determined for each region. For example, the fracture mode in the region-1 (near the surface) is mainly transgranular and the PIF-value is as low as 26%. In contrast, the fracture mode in the region-10 (at the center) is mainly intergranular and the PIF-value is as high as 90%. Fig. 1(b) shows the change in PIF-value as a function of distance from the surface.

In an inter-diffusion system between two elements such as molybdenum and carbon, the carbon concentration (c) in the matrix of molybdenum is expressed by the following equation [9]:
(1)
c /
c 0
= 1 − erf ( r / 2
D t
)
(2)
D =
D 0
exp ( − Q / R T )
where c 0 is the carbon solubility limit at the diffusion temperature (T), r, the distance from the specimen surface, D, the diffusivity, t, the diffusion time, D 0, a constant, Q, the activation energy for carbon diffusion and R is the gas constant. In this study the formation of a surface hard layer after carburization is negligible and thereby the carbon supply into the specimen is controlled mostly by the carbon diffusion in the molybdenum matrix. On condition that the diffusion temperature and the time are constant, the carbon concentration is only a function of distance from the specimen surface and it tends to decrease gradually with the distance.

Here we make the following assumptions [6]. In case the carbon concentration is small (c ≤ c 1), the grain boundaries are generally very weak and the fracture mode is mainly intergranular. In case the carbon concentration is large (c ≥ c 1 > c 2), in contrast, the grain boundaries are strengthened and the fracture mode becomes mainly transgranular (low PIF-value). This is the case for the carburized specimen. In case the carbon concentration is between c 1 and c 2, the fracture mode is just a mixture of intergranular and transgranular (middle PIF-value). In the above discussion the value c 1 is a constant regardless of the Ti addition. Consequently the change in PIF-value as a function of distance from the specimen surface is schematically shown in Fig. 2 . From this figure, the carbon diffusion distance is strictly defined by the distance r 1 which corresponds to a carbon concentration c 1. However, it is an experimental fact that the change in PIF-value as a function of distance from the surface generally displays a “S”-shaped curve. Thereby we define the carbon diffusion distance as a distance r * at which the PIF-value becomes 80% of the average PIF-value for the as-recrystallized specimen for convenience [6].

At the distance r * ≈ r 1 (c*≈ c 1), the following equation is used [6].
(3)
r *
/ 2
D t
= β
(4)
(
r *
)
2
= 4
β 2
D t
where β is a constant but it depends on the carbon solubility limit c 0. In this study it is assumed that c 0 is approximately constant in a narrow temperature range, since only limited data are available [7,10]. Using Eqs. (2) and (4), the following equations are derived:
(5)
(
r *
)
2
= 4
β 2
D 0
t
exp ( − Q / R T )
(6)
ln (
(
r *
)
2
) = − Q / R T + ln ( 4
β 2
D 0
t )
(7)
log (
(
r *
)
2
) = log
e ( − Q / R T ) + log ( 4
β 2
D 0
t )
From the Arrhenius plots of the carbon diffusion distance (r *)2 and the temperature T, the activation energy Q and the constant term log(4β 2 D 0 t) are obtained.

Fig. 3 shows the PIF-value as a function of distance from the surface for Mo–1.0Ti and Mo–1.5Ti after recrystallization. Each data point is an average of 4–6 values. The data for the as-recrystallized Pure Mo are also shown in the figure. The PIF-value are generally as high as 70% or more regardless of the distance from the surface, though there is a scattering in the data. The PIF-value for Mo–Ti alloys are generally lower than that for Pure Mo. Hiraoka et al. [11] proposed that the fracture mode (PIF-value) is expressed by the following equation.
(8)
PIF = k (
σ
TG
−
σ
IG
)
where k is the material constant, and σ TG and σ IG are the transgranular and intergranular fracture strengths, respectively. It is deduced that Ti addition might influence both fracture strengths, though a further discussion is not given in this text. According to the definition of Fig. 2, the average PIF-value of 75 and 72% are the values for the as-recrystallized Mo–1.0Ti and Mo–1.5Ti, respectively. Thereby, for example, it is decided that the carbon diffusion distance in Mo–1.0Ti after carburization is defined as the distance at which the PIF-value is as high as 60%.

Figs. 4–8
show the change in PIF-value as a function of distance from the specimen surface at diffusion temperatures between 1073 and 1473K. The region showing a low PIF-value generally progressed towards the center of specimen with the increase of temperature. However, the effect of Ti addition on the change in fracture mode as a function of distance from the specimen surface is distinct. First, after carburizing at 1073K (Fig. 4), the region near the surface in Mo–1.0Ti as well as in Pure Mo indicated a decrease in PIF-value, though the region near the center maintained a PIF-value as high as that for the as-recrystallized specimen. In contrast, Mo–1.5Ti after carburizing demonstrated no decrease in PIF-value even near the specimen surface. Secondly, after carburizing at 1273K (Fig. 6), the PIF-value for Pure Mo became relatively low regardless of the distance from the surface. In contrast, in Mo–Ti alloys, the region near the specimen center still maintained a PIF-value as high as that for the as-recrystallized specimen. Thirdly, after carburizing at 1473K (Fig. 8), the PIF-value became generally low regardless of the distance from the surface in Mo–1.0Ti as well as in Pure Mo. In contrast, in Mo–1.5Ti, the PIF-value near the specimen center was still as high as that for the as-recrystallized specimen.

From Figs. 3–8, it is obvious that Ti addition tends to decrease the carbon diffusion distance at a given temperature. Under some conditions, the carbon diffusion distance could not be determined, since a typical S-shaped curve was not obtained. Fig. 9 shows the Arrhenius plots of the carbon diffusion distance (r *)2 and the temperature. Almost a linear relationship was obtained for the carburized Mo–1.0Ti and Mo–1.5Ti. From the slope of the linear relationship, the activation energy Q was evaluated. Also by an extrapolation method, the constant term log(4β 2 D 0 t) was evaluated. The results are summarized in Table 1 . Both the activation energy and the constant term for Mo–Ti alloys were generally smaller than those for Pure Mo. The activation energy for Pure Mo in the table was determined from the original data [6] and the additional data, and is slightly larger than the previous value (182kJ). The activation energy is an indicator of the rate of carbon diffusion in the molybdenum matrix. Thereby the present results suggest that carbon atoms can diffuse more easily in Mo–Ti alloys than in Pure Mo. In this study the diffusion time is constant (1.2ks). Hence the constant term log(4β 2 D 0 t) is only a function of the value β, assuming the term D 0 is approximately constant. Using the constant D 0 =3.4mm2/s [7] and t =1.2ks, the constant β is derived and shown also in Table 1. The terms β of Mo–Ti alloys are generally smaller than that of Pure Mo. From Eqs. (1) and (3), the term β should be constant, if Ti addition has no effects on the carbon solubility limit c 0 and the critical carbon concentration c *. It is reasonable that a small addition of an alloy element may not change the solubility limit. Therefore, it is deduced that Ti addition increases the critical carbon concentration corresponding to the apparent carbon diffusion distance r *. The increase in critical carbon concentration by adding Ti is interpreted as follows. In Pure Mo, carbon atoms diffuse into the matrix during carburizing and the carbon atoms mostly segregate into the grain boundaries. In Mo–Ti alloys, on the other hand, carbon atoms also diffuse into the matrix. However, a part of the carbon atoms prefers to combine with Ti atoms, though rest of them still segregates into the grain boundaries. This suggests that Ti addition apparently increases the critical carbon concentration followed by a decrease in the value β. Anyway multiple changes of the activation energy for carbon diffusion and the critical carbon concentration by Ti addition lead to a decrease in carbon diffusion distance in molybdenum.

A small addition of Ti tends to decrease the carbon diffusion distance. From Arrhenius plots of the diffusion distance and the diffusion temperature, diffusion parameters such as the activation energy Q for carbon diffusion and the constant term log(4β 2 D 0 t) were obtained. These two parameters for Mo–Ti alloys are generally smaller than that for Pure Mo. The decrease in activation energy by Ti addition suggests that carbon diffusion is enhanced. On the other hand, the decrease in the constant term by Ti addition suggests an apparent increase in critical carbon concentration arising from the preferential combination between carbon atoms and Ti atoms.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Mr. Hiroaki Iwasawa, graduate student for his assistance with experiments.
